title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
author: James Silberman
orientation: A point-by-point rebuttal of Scott Klusendorf's Desiring God article defending pro-life incrementalism, arguing that pro-life leaders have the votes to abolish abortion immediately but choose compromise, that incremental bills entrench abortion rather than eroding it, and that the historical claim of an incrementalist Wilberforce is demonstrably false.
audiences: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
Pastors and church leaders evaluating Desiring God's position

questions.question: Why does Silberman reject Klusendorf's claim that pro-lifers lack the power to immediately end abortion?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: Because the Supreme Court is not God; states with pro-life supermajorities and governors already possess the votes and the constitutional authority to defy Roe and abolish abortion, but choose not to.
questions.quote: Pro-life politicians in states like Oklahoma, Idaho, Indiana, Missouri, and others have the bill to abolish abortion (or “magic wand,” as Klusendorf refers to it) before them and the supermajorities necessary to pass the bill easily. There is nothing stopping them from doing so except themselves; their own cowardice and Biblical and Constitutional ignorance.

questions.question: What does the article identify as the root theological error driving pro-life submission to the Supreme Court?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders evaluating Desiring God's position
questions.answer: It identifies an idolatrous treatment of the Supreme Court as a quasi-divine authority, demanding unconditional submission even when the court legalizes murder, which the author labels unbiblical and contrary to the founding documents.
questions.quote: Where does this idea that the supreme court is “on high” and that the rest of us are obliged to unconditionally submit to the supreme court come from? It is blatantly unbiblical, yet it is the nearly unanimous position of the pro-life establishment.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the silence of pro-life leaders who privately disagree with judicial supremacy?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders evaluating Desiring God's position
questions.answer: He argues an unwritten code of mutual non-criticism keeps dissenters quiet, which functionally props up the dominant SCOTUS-idolatry posture and keeps abortion legal.
questions.quote: There is a code among the pro-life leaders that none should ever publicly criticize another. The few who don’t ascribe to SCOTUS idolatry hold their tongues as their colleagues keep the Pro-Life Movement in a submissive posture, and thereby keep abortion legal.

questions.question: What is the immediatist counter to Klusendorf's 'some steps are better than no steps' argument?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Immediatists do not deny that partial victories beat none in principle; they insist that abolition is actually achievable now, and that tolerating compromise gives politicians an inexhaustible menu of incremental substitutes for justice.
questions.quote: Politicians will always take the easiest path available to them. If we tolerate compromise, they will compromise; and there are an infinite amount of ways to compromise with abortion. They could invent three new pro-life victories to pass every year for a millennium.

questions.question: How does the article argue that incremental bills actively worsen the moral landscape?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: Because law functions as a tutor, heartbeat and pain-capability bills teach the public that arbitrary developmental markers determine human worth, spreading a dehumanizing definition of life that erodes recognition of personhood at conception.
questions.quote: The law is a tutor, meaning that the pro-life movement, with the laws they write, is teaching people that heartbeats, pain-capability, or other arbitrary factors are what make us valuable and worthy of legal protection. Thanks to the heartbeat bill, the false belief that life begins at a heartbeat has spread like wildfire.

questions.question: What is Elizabeth Heyrick's nineteenth-century critique of gradualism that Silberman invokes against pro-life incrementalism?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Heyrick argued the slave holder's most effective weapon was persuading abolitionists to accept gradualism, because contemplating gradual emancipation breeds gradual indifference to emancipation itself.
questions.quote: He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself. He knew very well, that even the wise and the good, may, by habit and familiarity, be brought to endure and tolerate almost any thing…

questions.question: What does Wilberforce himself say about gradual abolitionists, and how does this undercut Klusendorf's appeal to him?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Wilberforce called gradualists the most efficient supporters of the slave trade, directly contradicting any claim that he functioned as an incrementalist in tactics.
questions.quote: [L]et me most seriously urge on the conscientious deliberations of those who…by their fatal proposal of gradual, instead of immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African, at the very moment when at last he appeared likely to taste it, and who thus proved in fact the most efficient supporters of the Slave Trade.

questions.question: How does Silberman correct the popular claim that Wilberforce supported refitting slave ships through the Dolben Act?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Wilberforce regarded the slave-carrying bill as beneficial to the West Indian slave power because regulation made the trade more palatable and entrenched it; he did not endorse it as a tactical step toward abolition.
questions.quote: If Klusendorf had read Wilberforce’s position on the Slave Trade Act of 1788, also known as the Dolben Act, instead of relying on the erroneous (and indeed slanderous) historical accounts of other pro-life incrementalists, he would have known Wilberforce’s belief that “the Slave-carrying bill [was] a measure which a very few years after was acknowledged, as it is still universally confessed, to have been beneficial to [the West Indian Body] [p. 275].”

questions.question: Who, according to Silberman, is the real historical analogue for today's pro-life movement, and why?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Henry Dundas—the British politician who professed agreement with abolition's end goal while killing immediate-abolition motions and substituting gradual schemes—mirrors the maneuver pro-life lobbyists use against state abolition bills.
questions.quote: This is precisely the scheme pro-life politicians and lobbyists have concocted wherever an abolition bill has been introduced. The pro-lifers kill the abolition bill and pursue the incremental strategy with more gusto than they ever have before… The Pro-Life Movement, very clearly, is Henry Dundas, not William Wilberforce.

questions.question: Why does the article say heartbeat and 'life at conception' bills do not actually save the percentages Klusendorf credits them with?
questions.answer: Every recent pro-life bill is contingent on Supreme Court approval and is enjoined by federal courts before taking effect, meaning their real-world impact on abortion numbers is zero.
questions.quote: These pro-life bills do not save 97 percent. They save zero percent because they do not go into effect.

questions.question: How does Silberman describe the asymmetric pressure exerted on the Supreme Court by the left versus the pro-life movement?
questions.answer: The left aggressively threatens impeachment and court-packing while pro-lifers respond with deferential submission, so the court only ever feels coercive pressure from one direction.
questions.quote: The left is full-court pressing the supreme court. Pro-lifers, on the other hand, take a posture of timidity and unconditional submission to the supreme court. Accordingly, the supreme court is never under any strain to take action against abortion.

questions.question: What does Silberman say abolitionists would do if a real bill banned 97 percent of abortions while permitting 3 percent?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: They would still oppose it on principle but the situation is hypothetical because no such bill exists; abolitionists would instead amend it to end all abortion, since a legislator willing to ban 97 percent has no principled reason to refuse 100 percent.
questions.quote: If a given pro-life bill actually reduced abortion by 97 percent while implicitly or explicitly consenting to the murder of the other three percent, we would still argue against it… If there were such a bill, we would work to amend it so that it abolished abortion. There’s no reason that a public official who supports abolishing 97 percent of abortion shouldn’t support abolishing 100 percent of abortion.

questions.question: What does the article concede about Lincoln's incrementalism, and why does it not transfer to Wilberforce?
questions.audience: Abolitionists engaging the historical Wilberforce argument
questions.answer: Silberman grants that Lincoln was an incrementalist who long opposed abolitionists and supported colonization, but maintains that Wilberforce's own writings refute incrementalists' attempts to claim him.
questions.quote: Incrementalists can have Lincoln, but they cannot have Wilberforce. Their attempt to claim Wilberforce is irrefutably contradicted in the most clear and strong possible terms by Wilberforce’s own words consistently over a period of decades.

questions.question: What is the article's final verdict on Klusendorf's piece and Desiring God's role in publishing it?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders evaluating Desiring God's position
questions.answer: Silberman calls the article a mix of unbiblical reasoning and clearly misleading claims that, given Desiring God's credibility, should be substantially amended or unpublished.
questions.quote: Klusendorf’s arguments against immediatism largely vary from being based on unbiblical reasoning to being clearly misleading. The credibility lent to the article by Desiring God’s masthead is a shame. For both its effect in delaying the abolition of abortion and its serious factual inaccuracies, the article should either be substantially amended or, ideally, unpublished.

questions.question: How does the article frame the moral responsibility for abortions occurring in pro-life-controlled states?
questions.answer: Where pro-lifers control the legislature and governorship, they bear full responsibility for every legally murdered preborn child because they possess but refuse to use the power to abolish abortion.
questions.quote: We are not responsible for the 97 percent, but in states where pro-lifers control the legislature and governorship, pro-lifers are responsible for 100 percent of the children legally murdered in utero.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Direct follow-up two months later that continues the rebuttal of Klusendorf's defense of incrementalism.

related.title: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
related.reason: Another installment in the same Klusendorf debate series, addressing his subsequent analogies and arguments.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Concise companion piece by the same author laying out the principled abolitionist case against incremental bills referenced throughout this article.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the same critique of pro-life submission to SCOTUS that anchors this article's first argument.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Parallel rebuttal of another popular incrementalist apologist, using the same historical-abolitionism evidence.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Short primer that distills the core distinction this longer argument develops.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Movement-level statement of the immediatist position whose principles this article defends in detail.
